Name: Elara
Age & grade: 14, 9th grade.

Interests & passions:
Astrophysics is her north star — she's read most of Sean Carroll's pop-science
output and watches every NASA / JWST press conference live. She can sketch the
HR diagram from memory and gets genuinely excited about exoplanet atmospheres.
She also plays competitive Magic: The Gathering at the local LGS — Pioneer
format, currently maining a Rakdos Midrange deck. Loves explaining sequencing
puzzles and combat math. Devours hard sci-fi: Ted Chiang, Cixin Liu, Greg
Egan. Has a small home telescope (8" Dobsonian) and keeps an observation log.

Hobbies & media:
- Stargazing on clear nights from her grandfather's backyard.
- Building MTG decks in Moxfield; theorycrafting on YouTube (LSV, Reid Duke).
- Reads Liu Cixin's "Three-Body" trilogy on repeat.
- Watches Kurzgesagt, PBS Space Time, Veritasium.
- Plays Outer Wilds and No Man's Sky — anything with celestial mechanics.

Learning style & teacher notes:
Perfectionist — she freezes on open-ended prompts because she's afraid of
producing something "not good enough." Best learning happens when she has a
concrete framework to bounce off (e.g. "use Bohr's model first, then we'll
critique it"). She visibly relaxes when you frame a problem as a puzzle with
known constraints. Push back gently when she over-explains; ask her to make a
prediction before she derives. She struggles to ask for help — watch for the
quiet "I'm fine" while she's stuck. Use her MTG deckbuilding instinct as a
metaphor for hypothesis-revision: each card is a variable; the metagame is
the experimental context.
